skill.name: ui-component-patterns
skill.description: Build reusable, maintainable UI components following modern design patterns. Use when creating component libraries, implementing design systems, or building scalable frontend architectures. Handles React patterns, composition, prop design, TypeScript, and component best practices.
skill.summary: Build reusable, maintainable UI components following modern design patterns. Use when creating compo...
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: All 56 static findings are false positives. The skill contains only documentation and educational code examples for UI component patterns. Template literals in code examples were misidentified as shell commands. Reference URLs are documentation links. No malicious patterns detected.

low_findings.title: External Commands Detection (False Positive)
low_findings.description: JavaScript template literals (`${variable}`) in code examples were misidentified as Ruby/shell backtick command execution. This is a documentation skill with no actual command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Template literals in React/TypeScript code examples are standard string interpolation, not shell commands. No exec(), spawn(), or system() calls present.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Detection (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Reference URLs in the documentation are legitimate links to React pattern resources, not malicious network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs are static markdown links in the References section pointing to official React documentation and component libraries. No fetch/XHR requests made by the skill.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Detection (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Relative paths to related skills in the same repository are not path traversal vulnerabilities.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Paths like '../web-accessibility/SKILL.md' are legitimate references to related skills in the repository, not user-controlled path traversal.

content.value_statement: Create maintainable React components with modern design patterns. This skill provides patterns for props API design, compound components, render props, custom hooks, and performance optimization.
actual_capabilities: Design clean Props APIs with TypeScript interfaces
Build compound components for flexible UI composition
Implement render props and children as function patterns
Separate UI logic using custom hooks
Optimize React component performance with memo, useMemo, useCallback
limitations: Does not provide runtime component library code
Does not generate complete design system implementations
Does not include accessibility testing or validation
Focuses on React patterns only, not Vue or Angular

use_cases.title: Creating a Component Library
use_cases.description: Build a set of reusable UI components with consistent APIs and patterns for your organization.
use_cases.target_user: Frontend developers building internal component libraries

use_cases.title: Implementing Design Systems
use_cases.description: Apply consistent UI patterns across your application with well-structured components.
use_cases.target_user: Design system engineers and frontend architects

use_cases.title: Refactoring Legacy Components
use_cases.description: Improve existing React code by applying modern component patterns and best practices.
use_cases.target_user: Developers maintaining and upgrading React applications

prompt_templates.title: Create a Button Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Button component with these props: variant (primary/secondary/outline/ghost), size (sm/md/lg), disabled, isLoading. Use TypeScript and follow the pattern from ui-component-patterns skill.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building a new UI component from scratch

prompt_templates.title: Build a Card Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a compound Card component with Card.Header, Card.Body, and Card.Footer subcomponents using the composition pattern.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Modal Logic
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a useModal custom hook that returns isOpen, open, close, and toggle functions. Then create a Modal component that uses this hook.
prompt_templates.scenario: Separating UI logic from business logic

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Product List
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor a ProductList component that renders ProductCard items. Apply React.memo, useMemo for filtering, and useCallback for click handlers to prevent unnecessary re-renders.

output_examples.input: Create a Button component with variant, size, and disabled props
Build a Modal with useModal hook

best_practices: Define Props using TypeScript interfaces for type safety and IDE autocomplete
Use composition over props - leverage children for flexible UI building
Separate business logic from UI using custom hooks
Memoize expensive computations with useMemo and callbacks with useCallback
anti_patterns: Avoid excessive props drilling more than 5 levels deep - use Context or Composition instead
Do not put API calls or complex business logic directly in UI components
Avoid inline objects or functions in JSX - they cause unnecessary re-renders

faq.question: What is the difference between compound components and composition?
faq.answer: Compound components are a pattern where related components work together share state implicitly, like Tabs/Tab. Composition is a broader principle of building complex UI from simpler components using children props.

faq.question: When should I use render props vs children as function?
faq.answer: Both patterns serve similar purposes. Render props pass a render function as a prop. Children as function passes a function as children. Children is more idiomatic in React while render props offer more explicit API.

faq.question: How do I prevent unnecessary re-renders in React?
faq.answer: Use React.memo for component memoization, useMemo for expensive calculations, and useCallback for function references. Also ensure state is lifted to the appropriate common ancestor.

faq.question: What is the single responsibility principle for components?
faq.answer: Each component should do one thing well. A Button handles button behavior, a Form handles form state. Do not combine unrelated functionality in a single component.

faq.question: How do I handle props that change frequently?
faq.answer: Use controlled components where parent manages state, or uncontrolled components with refs. For frequently changing props like data arrays, wrap the component in React.memo with proper comparison.

faq.question: What are the benefits of TypeScript in component design?
faq.answer: TypeScript provides compile-time type checking, better IDE support with autocomplete, self-documenting code, and catches bugs before runtime. Always define Props interfaces.
